title: Water Quality: A Foundation for Health and Development

hero.title: Water Quality: A Foundation for Health and Development
hero.lead: Access to safe drinking water is essential for health and development, yet over 2 billion people still lack it. Scroll to climb the service ladder — from surface water to safely managed.

blocks.title: A quarter of the world’s population lacks access to safe drinking water
blocks.subtitle: Five levels of drinking water services (% of population)
blocks.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP for Water Supply, Sanitation and Hygiene. Retrieved from World Development Indicators (SH.H2O.SMDW.ZS).

scenes.text: [emphasis: Safely managed]: water from an improved source that is available on premises, when needed, and free from fecal and priority chemical contamination. Global access rose from 61 percent in 2000 to 74 percent in 2024 — yet 2.1 billion people still lack safe drinking water.

[emphasis: Basic]: from an improved source, often off-premises but within a 30-minute round trip, without guaranteed water quality. In 2024 about 18 percent of the world (1.4 billion people) had this level of service.
One-tenth of the global population lacked even basic drinking water in 2024: [emphasis: limited], [emphasis: unimproved], or [emphasis: surface] water from rivers, dams, and lakes.

blocks.html: Climbing the ladder takes decades. Progress is uneven across regions — and where people live still shapes whether water is safely managed.

blocks.title: Access to safely managed drinking water remains much lower in rural areas
blocks.subtitle: People using safely managed drinking water services, by residence (% of population)
blocks.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP. WDI rural / urban.

scenes.text: While urban access to [emphasis: safely managed] water stayed just over 80 percent from 2000 to 2024, rural access rose from about 40 percent to 60 percent — yet a global urban–rural gap of roughly 20 percentage points remains. South Asia narrowed the gap; Sub-Saharan Africa rural coverage is still only about 17 percent.

blocks.html: National averages hide another truth: many countries still have very low coverage — especially in Sub-Saharan Africa and some small island economies.

blocks.title: Access to safely managed drinking water is low in Sub-Saharan Africa and small island economies
blocks.subtitle: People using safely managed drinking water services, 2024 (% of population)

scenes.text: Data are available for 148 countries for 2024. Roughly one-third of countries still lack sufficient water monitoring or testing systems for this indicator.
Among countries with data, only 29 have achieved universal access (99 percent or higher) to safely managed drinking water.

Low access is concentrated in Sub-Saharan Africa, where less than half the population has safe drinking water in 22 of 25 countries with data. Some Pacific island economies also remain near the bottom of the distribution.

blocks.html: Safely managed water is not a single pipe — it is the minimum of three components: water on premises, available when needed, and free from contamination.

blocks.title: Safely managed access is determined by the lowest of three components
blocks.subtitle: Share of people with improved water meeting each component (%)

scenes.text: In [emphasis: Mongolia], accessibility on premises is the bottleneck: only 43 percent have water on premises, while availability (84%) and freedom from contamination (82%) are much higher. Safely managed equals the minimum — 43 percent.

In [emphasis: Nepal], the limiting factor is quality: accessibility and availability are near 80 percent, but only 16 percent have water free from contamination — so safely managed access is 16 percent.

blocks.html: Different countries hit different ceilings. Some struggle with quality, others with accessibility or availability when needed.

blocks.title: Countries face different challenges in achieving safely managed drinking water
blocks.subtitle: Accessibility, availability, and quality components (% of population)
blocks.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP · World Bank staff calculations.

scenes.text: In several Pacific Island nations — including Tuvalu, Nauru, and Tonga — water [emphasis: quality] is the binding constraint even when accessibility looks high.
[emphasis: Accessibility] (water on premises) is a major challenge in many Sub-Saharan African countries such as the Central African Republic and Chad.
Water is [emphasis: available when needed] if households report sufficient supply. For some economies this is the weakest of the three pillars.
Across 148 countries with data, the limiting factor varies. Highlighted bars show the binding component that sets safely managed access for each economy.

blocks.html: Over time, countries often expand accessibility faster than they improve quality. Tanzania shows the pattern clearly — and it is not unique.

blocks.title: Progress in water quality lags availability and accessibility
blocks.subtitle: People with access to each component of safely managed water (% of population)
blocks.source: WHO/UNICEF JMP Global Database; World Bank staff calculations.

scenes.text: In 2000, nearly 15 percent of people in [emphasis: Tanzania] had access to uncontaminated water, but only about 1 percent had water on premises.

Over the next two decades, accessibility improved dramatically, while gains in water quality were more limited. By 2024 nearly half the population had water on premises, yet only about one-third had uncontaminated water.
Is this unique to Tanzania? Dashed lines show a “typical” trajectory of improvement estimated from historical country-level data for each component.
On the typical path, raising water accessibility from 40 percent to 75 percent would take about 33 years.
Achieving the same scale of progress in water quality would take about 101 years — roughly three times longer.

blocks.html:
Safe water is a foundation for health and development. Closing gaps means investing not only in pipes and pumps, but also in monitoring, treatment, and services that keep water clean from source to cup.

Water story 6/11 library blocks. Remaining origin chapters: E. coli table/scatter, urban–rural E. coli, Bangladesh arsenic.
